topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

The spreadsheet glowed accusingly on my monitor, the numbers swimming before my eyes. I had been meticulously tracking project allocations for the last three weeks, a task I usually approached with surgical precision. Then, I saw it: a direct comparison of salaries, hidden in a poorly formatted internal report. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake of air that felt too loud in the quiet office. I slowly lowered my gaze from the screen, my hands beginning to tremble slightly as I gripped my mouse. It was Sarah. Sarah, who had just finished her first big presentation and was already being praised by management. The reality of the discrepancy settled over me like a heavy blanket, making my stomach clench tight. I fumbled for my coffee mug, the ceramic feeling suddenly slick in my sweaty palm, and retreated to my desk, pretending to be deeply engrossed in an unrelated document.

[story 2]

"Just a quick query on the Q3 budget projections, Mark?" Liam’s voice was casual, entirely unaware of the internal earthquake happening within me. I managed a strained, "Yeah, sure, just a moment." My fingers hovered over the keyboard, unable to type a coherent response. The data had been undeniable, starkly clear on the screen. It wasn't a misunderstanding; it was a verifiable fact. A wave of heat rushed up my neck, making my skin prickle uncomfortably. I forced a smile, a brittle thing that didn't reach my eyes, and pointed vaguely toward the projection. "Well, based on these figures..." The words came out thin and reedy. I could feel the eyes of everyone in the open-plan office on me, and the urge to simply vanish into the beige partition wall was overwhelming.

[story 3]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred into meaningless shapes. The numbers—the tangible proof of my own perceived inadequacy—seemed to mock me. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my molars. Every sound in the open office seemed amplified: the tapping of keyboards, the distant hum of the air conditioning. I needed to move, to escape the suffocating spotlight of my own realization. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, a noise that seemed to draw immediate attention. I walked toward the water cooler, my movements jerky and unnatural, trying to regulate the frantic drumming beneath my ribs. I grabbed a bottle of water, the cold plastic offering a brief, sharp distraction from the dizzying sense of being fundamentally outmatched.
